course_title: Understanding Mental Health and Psychotherapeutic Interventions
course_description:
This course is designed to provide you with the foundation knowledge components required for critically engaging with the mental health field and progressing to more advanced courses. The course provides an overview of the field including the history and philosophy of mental illness and treatment from different perspectives. Different systems of diagnostic assessment across the psychiatric and psychotherapeutic approaches will be explored. Contemporary treatment approaches will then be reviewed and critiqued including the role of medications in the treatment of mental health issues. The role of evidence-based treatments will be emphasised throughout the course and you will have opportunities to critique the strengths and weaknesses of this approach. You will be required to choose one treatment approach and diagnostic issue covered in the course to engage with in your assessment tasks.

Welcome to PXMH7101, the first course in the Master of Mental Health and Graduate Certificate in Mental Health programs.

This course is designed to provide you with knowledge components required to critically engageﾠwith the mental health field and as a foundation for more advanced courses. The course provides an overview of the field including the history and philosophy of mental illness and treatment from a number of different perspectives. Different systems of diagnostic assessment across the psychiatric andﾠpsychotherapeutic approaches will be explored. Contemporary treatment approaches will then be reviewed and critiqued including the role of medications in the treatment of mental health issues. The role of evidence-based treatments will be emphasised throughout the course and you will have opportunity to critique the strengths and weaknesses of this approach.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
TUTORIALS
Tutorials will be held in
each week
of the semester starting Week 1 (23-27 February 2026) to Week 13 (25-29 May), except for the workshop week in week 3 (9-13 March) and the mid-semester break (6-10 April). You should attend the tutorial group of your Field of Study (Art Therapy or Psychotherapy). You are expected to attend tutorials regularly, complete the required reading and contribute to the discussion in the tutorial.
Art Therapy
(students enrolled in Master of Mental Health Art Therapy):
1. Remote students: Tuesdays 16:00-17:15pm with Manaali Manoharan. You will attend using Zoom.
2. Local students: Tuesdays 14:30-15:45 pm with Manaali Manoharan. You will attend using Zoom. ﾠ
Psychotherapy
ﾠ (students enrolled in Master of Mental Health Psychotherapy, and Grad Cert Mental Health):
ﾠTuesdays 5:30-6:45 pm with Sam McArdle. You will attend using Zoom
WORKSHOPS
These in-person all-day workshops, scheduled for Tuesday 10 March 2026, are held at the Herston campus in Brisbane. They are opportunities to work with your peers on key knowledge areas and clinical skills with direction from experienced UQ staff.
You are expected to attend the workshops
. If you have difficulties attending the workshops you should contact the course coordinator.
For more details see Learning Activities.
LECTURES
Some recorded lectures will be posted to the PXMH7101 Learn.UQ (Blackboard Ultra) site. They will be available to play when it is convenient for you.
course_aims: The course will provide an overview of contemporary approaches to mental health, mental illness and mental health interventions and will consider both historicalﾠ practice and future directions.ﾠIt will critically examineﾠcurrent approaches to treatment and evidence based practice.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Understand and critique contemporary and historical approaches to mental health and mental illness.
Understand and critique contemporary and historical systems of diagnosis in mental health and psychotherapy.
Understand and critique contemporary treatment approaches in mental health and psychotherapy practice.
Critically understand the role of evidence-based practice in mental health and psychotherapy, both current and developing.

assessment_summary.title: Examining Evidence-Based Practice
assessment_summary.due_date: 25/05/2026 - 5/06/2026; Specific dates of presentations to be negotiated with tutors.; To be video recorded as per UQ policy on oral assessments.

assessment_details.title: Mental Health and Mental Illness
assessment_details.task_description: PXMH7101: Assignment 1 Mental Health and Mental Illness Both mental health and mental illness are contested terms. Articulate your current understanding of the most appropriate ways of defining these terms considering authoritative published guidelines, professional and research literature, experiences of people who have experienced mental illness diagnosis and treatment and other sources which you consider contribute to your current understanding of these terms. Contrast your understanding with a perspective shared by a colleague during the workshops. This essay should demonstrate your capacity to critically examine a range of sources of information about a contested topic and articulate, in an academic format, your own position. Essay is 1500 words (+/-10%), formatted in an appropriate academic style and using APA 7th referencing. Submission via Turnitin. Criteria and Marking Student has presented their own current understanding of mental health and mental illness. Student has demonstrated capacity to critically examine key sources such as authoritative published guidelines, professional and research literature, and lived experience accounts, and integrated information from these sources into their own understanding of these terms. Student contrasts their personal understandings with a specific example of a perspective voiced by a colleague during the workshop Essay is 1500 words (+/-10%), is written in an appropriate academic format and uses APA 7th referencing Assignment Weighting: 30% Please consult with staff for legitimate use of AI for this essay. The course coordinator or tutor might organise an oral examination of the essay to confirm authorship and verify learnings acquired through its elaboration. Please check https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/tools-and-techniques/ai-student-hub/ai-for-assessment Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Diagnosis in Mental Health Practice
assessment_details.task_description: Task: Diagnosis in mental health practice is used to communicate with other professionals and to guide the treatment of mental health problems. Diagnostic systems are criticised on a range of fronts, not least of which is that categorical systems are frequently a poor fit for painting an accurate picture of an individual’s personal experience of mental illness or psychological distress. The most dominant system of diagnosis for mental health is the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-V-TR). Select a specific diagnosis from the DSM-V-TR (e.g. Generalised Anxiety Disorder, not ‘anxiety’). Write a 2500-word analytical essay exploring how the diagnostic approach of the DSM-V-TR assists, or does not assist in: understanding the difficulties which have brought an individual into treatment for this condition, and determining the most appropriate pathway forward. Contrast your findings with an alternative nosological approach (for instance, the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual [PDM-2]) and from your research develop a compelling conclusion as to the role of diagnosis in mental health practice. Please consult with staff for legitimate use of AI for this essay. The course coordinator or tutor might organise an oral examination of the essay to confirm authorship of the essay and verify learnings acquired through its elaboration. Please check https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/tools-and-techniques/ai-student-hub/ai-for-assessment Criteria & Marking: 1. Using the diagnostic criteria, evaluate how the nosological approach of the DSM-V-TR assists or does not assist in understanding the difficulties which have brought an individual into treatment for your chosen condition, and determining the most appropriate pathway forward 2. Compare and contrast your findings with the approach taken by an appropriate alternative nosological framework in understanding the symptoms and personal experience of the consumer with your chosen condition 3. Using your experience of evaluating these diagnostic approaches, as well as literature on diagnosis from a research, clinical and lived-experience perspective, present your critical evaluation of the role of diagnosis in mental health practice. 5. Essay is in an appropriate academic form and structure, uses formatting that assists in clarifying the evidence and the argument relying on that evidence, is well written and free from grammatical and spelling errors, uses a standard referencing format and is 2500 words (+/- 10%). Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Examining Evidence-Based Practice
assessment_details.due_date: 25/05/2026 - 5/06/2026 Specific dates of presentations to be negotiated with tutors. To be video recorded as per UQ policy on oral assessments.
assessment_details.task_description: Evidence-based practice underpins contemporary mental health treatment. The argument is that if empirical research has supported the efficacy of a treatment, it must be effective. This position is flawed as empirical research typically demonstrates reliable clinical change only for a percentage of consumers. In essence, no treatment works for everyone to the same degree. Choose a diagnostic disorder from the DSM-V-TR (NOT "Depression") and examine the research evidence to assist in deciding about appropriate treatment for this disorder. You should compare two different psychotherapy-based treatment approaches for your chosen disorder. You can choose any two different approaches as long as they have been subject to empirical investigation and the differences between them can be clearly articulated. It is suggested that your chosen treatment approaches should each have a minimum of 4 primary (original data, with consumers) studies to refer to. Art Therapy Students: Art Therapy students should present on research into an Art Therapy based modality specifically for their chosen disorder. Given the limited pool of research into specific, disorder focused modalities in Art Therapy, students may consider evaluating art-based approaches as a whole for their specific condition. Develop a 10-minute presentation for your colleagues and tutor on your findings, including a critical discussion assessing which approach may be most effective and why. Your critical discussion should include an assessment of common factors and shared mechanisms of change across your chosen modalities which might explain equivocal or disparate effectiveness. You will then facilitate a 5 minute discussion with the class addressing any questions arising from your presentation. What can be concluded about best EBP with your chosen disorder based on your review? Criteria & Marking: 1. Student identifies an appropriate DSM-V disorder and identifies two appropriate treatment approaches that have been subject to empirical investigation and the differences between them can be clearly articulated. 2. Student compares and critically evaluates the two different treatment approaches for the chosen disorder and reviews at least 4 studies for each approach (a mix of primary research and meta-analysis or meta-synthesis is acceptable if said reviews explore primary research). 3. Student discusses possible common factors or specific mechanisms of change which might explain equivocal, or disparate treatment efficacy (depending on findings) 4. Student develops conclusions about evidence-based practice with the chosen disorder based on the review. 5. Student has prepared and presented an engaging, informative and thoughtful presentation lasting for 10 minutes and was able to participate authoritatively and clearly in a discussion with colleagues for a minimum of 5 minutes Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Overview of PXMH7101 Tutorial Question: What is PXMH7101 about and how can I have the best learning experience? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Mental Health & Wellbeing What are the dominant discourses on mental health and wellbeing, and which psychological, social, cultural and spiritual perspectives are marginalised? How does our conceptualisation of mental health influence our view of mental illness, if there is a relationship at all? Learning outcomes: L01

PXMH7101: Workshop Navigating historical and contemporary views on mental health and mental illness Exploring psychiatric nosology - the DSM and beyond Applying our learnings in understanding "depression" and it's treatment Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Historical & Contemporary Understandings of Mental Illness How have we defined and understood "mental illness" in the past? Is there value in understanding how and why these ideas became dominant before being left behind? What has changed about how we approach mental illness in contemporary Australian society? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

On the Origins and Nature of 'Mental Illness' Well, is it nature OR nurture? Is this the right question? What impact does the experience of trauma, oppression and poverty have on mental health and mental illness? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

Diagnostic Systems Part 1 What are the core assumptions of categorical diagnostic systems like the DSM and ICD? How did these systems come to dominate the field of psychiatric nosology? What are the strengths and weaknesses of categorical diagnostic systems? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

Diagnostic Systems Part 2 What are the alternatives to categorical taxonomic systems? What can we learn from the dimensional, prototypical or reseach models such as the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP), the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM- 2), Network Theory (NT) or the Research Domain Criteria (RDOC)? What about systems which eschew diagnosis altogether, such as the Power, Threat and Meaning Framwork (PTMF)? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Evidence-Based Practice Part 1: The bio-psycho-social model Is the "bio/psycho/social approach" discussed more than it is practiced? What are the alternatives? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Evidence-Based Practice Part 2: Only path or one of many? Where has the "evidence-based practice" movement taken us in the field of psychotherapy? What do we really mean when we say we are working from an "evidence-based perspective" and when does this language become disingenuous? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Medication in Mental Health Practice Why is medication the dominant response to mental health problems in Australia? Do the costs outweight the benefit? Learning outcomes: L03, L04
Psychotherapy in Mental Health: evidence of value What does the evidence tell us about the value of the psychotherapies for responding to mental health problems? Learning outcomes: L03, L04
Psychotherapy in Mental Health: evidence of risk What does the evidence tell us about the risks of the use of the psychotherapies for responding to mental health problems? Learning outcomes: L03, L04
Student Presentations As per task description in Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L03, L04
